english: (Lexx. have a pf. bkoṅ (bkong)) 1. c.c. daṅ (dang), to bear a grudge or ill-will against a person, to be dissatisfied with a thing; _kón-nas ('khon nas) when they (the states) were at war with one another (opp. to mťún-nas (mthun nas) in peaceful relations) Glr.; _kón-med-par ('khon med par) honestly, without insidious intentions, e.g. in negotiations Glr.; čos daṅ _kón-pa (chos dang 'khon pa) to wish to keep aloof from religion, or to have done with religion; in a special sense: to be tired of the clerical profession Glr.; _kón-żugs-pa ('khon zhugs pa), _kón-du ḍzin-pa ('khon du 'dzin pa) = _kón-pa ('khon pa); _kon res byėď-pa ('khon res byed pa) Sch. to quarrel, prob. more accurately, to have a spite against each other. - _kón-po ('khon po) dissension, discord Sch. Cf. kon-pa (khon pa). - 2. C. = _kar-ba ('khar ba) II.

Sch. barbarous, rough, rude, gen. combined with mťa (mtha'), mťa-_kóḃ (mtha' 'khob), with or without yul (yul), barbarous border-country. So the Tibetans always designate their own country, in comparison with India, the holy land of Buddhism, as being mťa-_kóḃ kla-klo-čaṅ (mtha' 'khob kla klo can). The rarely occurring yaṅ-kóḃ (yang khob) seems to indicate a still more distant and barbarous country.
to be startled, agitated, alarmed, in one Lex. however, it is explained by kŷáḃ-pa (khyab pa).

1. circle, circumference; the persons or objects encircling, surrounding (a certain point or place); lté-ba daṅ deí _kór-rnams (lte ba dang de'i 'khor rnams) the navel and the circumjacent parts Med.; *de-_kór-la* (de 'khor la) W. thereabout; nye-_kór (nye 'khor) v. nye (nye); more esp. retinue, attendants, _kór daṅ bčas-pa ('khor dang bcas pa) (सपरिवार) with attendants, suite: _kór rnám-pa ḃżi ('khor rnam pa bzhi) Tar. frq., the attendants of Buddha's hearers, divided into four classes (viz. in the earliest times: dge-slóṅ (dge slong), dge-slóṅ-ma (dge slong ma), dge-snyén (dge snyen), and dge-snyén-ma (dge snyen ma); at a later period: nyan-ťós (nyan thos), raṅ-saṅs-rgyás (rang sangs rgyas), byaṅ-čuḃ-séms-dpa (byang chub sems dpa'), and so-sói skyė-bo-rnams (so so'i skye bo rnams) q.v.) _kór dgra-bčóm-pas bskór-te ('khor dgra bcom pas bskor te) surrounded by the retinue of the Arhants (v. dgra-bčóm-pa (dgra bcom pa)); _kór-du bsdús-so ('khor du bsdus so) he gathered them round himself as his retinue Dzl.; also fig.: the train of thoughts, reminiscences etc., which the soul, when passing into a new body, cannot take along with it Thgy.; it is also used for a single servant or attendant (Cs. has _kór-po ('khor po) or _kór-pa ('khor pa) male attendant, and _kór-ma ('khor ma) female attendant), _kór gčig ('khor gcig) Mil.; _kór gnyis ('khor gnyis) two attendants Glr., hence _kór-rnams ('khor rnams) sometimes for domestics, household servants; but if _kór ('khor) with a numeral is preceded by gtsó-bo (gtso bo), or a similar noun, this preceding word is acc. to the Tibetan mode of speaking included in the number given, so that gtsó-bo _kór lṅa (gtso bo 'khor lnga) should be translated: the master and four attendants (not five). - 2. instead of _kór-ba ('khor ba), or _kór-lo ('khor lo), esp. in compound words; lo-_kór (lo 'khor) = lo-skór (lo skor) a cycle, comprising a space of twelve years. Comp. _kór-mkán ('khor mkhan) attendants Glr. - _kór-_báṅs ('khor 'bangs) subjects Cs. - _kór-gyóg ('khor g.yog) = _kór ('khor) retinue, servants etc. - *dhuṅ-kór* (dhung 'khor) C. waiting man, valet de chamber, = sku-mdún-pa (sku mdun pa) which is the respectful word for it. - naṅ-_kór (nang 'khor) household servants, domestics. - *kor-gyág* (khor gyag) W. latch. - _kór-_dás ('khor 'das) v. sub _kór-ba ('khor ba) II.

I. vb. (cf. skór-ba (skor ba)), to turn round, to turn about, to go round in a circle: _kór-gin yod ('khor gin yod) he is walking (running, flying etc.) round the . . . Glr.; esp. of the successive transmigrations of metempsychosis, v. II: mgo _kór (mgo 'khor) my head turns, I am getting dizzy, confused; also I am duped, cheated, imposed on, kŷéď-kyi ka-sbyáṅ-gyis ṅed mgo mi _kór (khyed kyi kha sbyang gyis nged mgo mi 'khor) we are not to be taken in by the volubility of your tongue Mil.; to pass away, to grow full, to be completed, lo-dús _kór-ba-na (lo dus 'khor ba na) when one year was past Glr.; srás-kyis lo _kór-te (sras kyis lo 'khor te) when the prince had completed one year, was one year old; *da ḃu-lon kor* (da bu lon khor) W. now the debt is entirely paid off, cleared; *kor mi ťub* (khor mi thub) it cannot be paid off; *mi kor* (mi khor), the sum is not full, not sufficient to cover the debt; to walk about, roam, ramble W.; to return from a journey, to come home; *raṅ-la kór-ba* (rang la 'khor ba) to come or fall back (on the head of the author, originator); to come together, to contract, to gather, e.g.
